1.text: Morphological Features of a Russian Freshwater Goby, Rhinogobius lindbergi (Pisces: Gobiidae), and Its Genetic Relationships to Japanese Species
Harumi Sakai¹, Ken Ikoma¹, Sergei V. Frolov², Yuji Yamazaki³, Hiroshi Takahashi⁴ and Hitoshi Ida⁵

¹Department of Applied Aquabiology, National Fisheries University, Nagatahonmachi, Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi 759-6595, Japan (e-mail: sakaih@fish-u.ac.jp)
²Institute of Marine Biology, Far East Branch, Russian Academy of Science, Vladivostok 690041, Russia
³Ocean Research Institute, University of Tokyo, 1-15-1 Minamidai, Nakano-ku, Tokyo 164-8639, Japan
⁴Faculty of Fisheries, Hokkaido University, Hakodate 041-8611, Japan
⁵School of Fisheries Sciences, Kitasato University, Sanriku, Kesen, Iwate 022-0101, Japan

Abstract. Russian freshwater goby Rhinogobius lindbergi from the Amur River basin is the northernmost species of the genus. It is diagnosed from about 50 congeners by the following combination of characteristics: preopercular canal with two open pores, vertebrae 27, pectoral fin rays 19-20, no predorsal scales, and a distinct dark spot between the 1st and the 2nd rays of the first dorsal fin in male. Whether R. lindbergi is a fluvial or an amphidromous species is unknown. It is also probable that R. lindbergi yields small floating larvae, but they never go to the sea. Allozyme comparisons with seven Japanese congeners indicate that this species is genetically less related to them, being slightly closer to an amphidromous species, Rhinogobius sp. LD "Oo-yoshinobori" sensu Kawanabe & Mizuno (1989).
Key words: Far East, Amur River, electrophoresis, allozyme, genetic distance, cephalic lateralis, amphidromy.

The Far Eastern freshwater goby Rhinogobius Gill, 1859, comprises about 50 species (Chen et al., 1999a), exhibiting an amphidromous or a fluvial mode of life. Their taxonomy and speciation problem involving the differentiation of the fluvial species have drawn much attention in Japan (Mizuno, 1987; Masuda et al., 1989; Shimizu et al., 1993; Katoh & Nishida, 1994; Nishida, 1994; Aonuma et al., 1998), Korea (Jeon & Aonuma, 1995), China (Chen et al., 1999b), Taiwan Island (Chen & Shao, 1996), Thailand and Laos (Chen et al., 1999a), etc.

Russian R. lindbergi (Berg, 1933) from the Amur River basin (Fig. 1) is the northernmost species of the genus. As the mouth of the Amur River opens to east (see Fig. 2), it may be possible that R. lindbergi has closer relationship to the Japanese congeners than others, having an amphidromous origin. Another possibility may be that R. lindbergi is a fluvial species and it had invaded the Amur basin from Chinese rivers through such a tributary change event as

In mainland Japan, one fluvial species, R. flumineus (Mizuno, 1960) "Kawa-yoshinobori", one amphidromous species, R. giurinus (Rutter, 1897) "Gokuraku-haze", and five amphidromous species of the so-called R. brunneus (Temminck & Schlegel, 1845) species group, namely, R. sp. CB "Shima-yoshinobori", R. sp. DA "Kuro-yoshinobori", R. sp. LD "Oo-yoshinobori", R. sp. CO "Ruri-yoshinobori", and R. sp. OR "Tou-yoshinobori" are known (sensu Kawanabe & Mizuno, 1989; Akihito et al., 1993).

In the first place to know the derivation of R. lindbergi, we observe its morphological features, examine its allozyme allelic composition, compare with those of the Japanese congeners, and try to estimate the above first possibility of relationships between R. lindbergi and the Japanese mainland congeners.
Specimens of Rhinogobius lindbergi were collected from some tributaries of the Amur River basin (Table 1, Fig. 2), and all the Japanese species were caught from rivers of Yamaguchi Prefecture,
